BTCfi infrastructure updates are outpacing new product launches.

Here are the biggest ecosystem updates from July ↓

→ @BitGo added support for @Stacks sBTC
This gives ~1.2M users a direct route between BTC and Stacks inside their existing custody setup, just before self-custodial Bitcoin Staking goes live.

→ @TRowePrice_DA launched TKNZ (an active crypto ETF)
This is a sign big institutions are moving from holding BTC to actively allocating across crypto.

→ @Strike launched BTC-backed loans without automatic liquidations
Borrowers can keep the loan open through price drops as long as they continue making payments.

→ @SolvProtocol expanded BTCFi in Asia
Metaplanet is exploring BTC-backed credit, while banks are starting to test similar products.

→ Bitcoin developers debated BIP-110 and BIP-361
One focuses on limiting unwanted data on Bitcoin. The other looks at protecting the network from future quantum attacks.

I’d argue that BitGo’s support for sBTC is the biggest update here, since custody access makes it much easier to put idle BTC to work.

Right now, access matters more than novelty in BTCfi.

The weirdest thing to think about:

I'm now almost 10 years into crypto, and there are things happening today that I wouldn't have even dared to wish for back when I started. Where we are as an industry has by far exceeded my expectations of where I thought we could ever go.

And yet, interest and sentiment have never been worse in my entire time here. Doesn't matter who I talk to, crypto natives or TradFi investors, nobody gives a shit about crypto anymore. Everyone is just chasing the next hot thing, whether it's AI, robotics, defense, biotech or whatever comes next.

I get the herd dynamic and how everyone just blindly chases the money. But knowing even the most basic principles of investing, how is this not the single worst time imaginable to turn your back on crypto?

I'll either turn out to be the biggest idiot for staying long-term bullish, or the biggest chad for being one of the few who never gave up after all these years.

But at least I get to fall asleep at night with a clear conscience, knowing I'm not as average as everyone else just blindly following the herd.

Believe in something.

Stock perps grew 65% last month while the overall Crypto perp DEX volume fell 24%.

Their share of total volume more than doubled from 5.8% to 12.5%.

At first I thought it was SpaceX’s IPO driving this, but it’s not. SpaceX contributed just 3% of the total increase in stock-perp volume.

So, what actually drove it? Three memory and storage stocks:

• SK Hynix: 53% of the increase
• Micron: 15.6%
• Sandisk: 15%

Together, they accounted for roughly 84% of the growth.

The stock-perp boom wasn’t broad-based demand for onchain equities. Crypto traders found another way to trade the AI memory boom, 24/7 with leverage.

I started with an “onchain Robinhood” thesis. That was too broad.

Perp DEXs are becoming leverage rails for whatever speculative trade has the most heat, even when the underlying stocks trade in Korea or the US.

thread_tweets.text:
Then, we had a series of three eras of advancement, in which machines rapidly gained areas of capabilities that far exceeded our own:

* In the industrial revolution, mass manufacturing, or generally the ability to perform the exact same action millions of times
* With calculators and computers, the ability to do mental tasks that can be defined via an analytic-philosophy-style logical definition (eg. computer code)
* With LLMs, the ability to do mental tasks (and physical tasks, eg. already self-driving cars) that can be defined via a large set of examples

But even after all three waves, at least today, there is still a large range of capabilities that remain unique to humans.

A big question facing us is: is the LLM wave "the real deal", in the sense that, as they keep getting better, they will eventually cover either all missing capabilities, or at least enough capabilities that the rest can be worked around, or are LLMs simply a continuation of the same pattern, and it will take some fourth or fifth wave to go the rest of the way?

thread_tweets.text:
In the previous wave (calculators and computers), many people thought "well if AI can solve hard things like 10x10 digit multiplication and quadratic equations so much better than humans can, then surely it will very soon also do easy things like telling a cat from a dog". As it turned out, they were wrong. This time, we're seeing the same argument replay - except this time there's much stronger arguments for the "LLMs plus a few more improvements can do everything" side, we just don't know yet if those arguments are strong enough that that will turn out to be true.

I feel like the utopia that "AI acceleration optimists" have in mind comes from a position of confidence that, while current wave of LLMs + agents unlock many new capabilities, there is still a significant range of capabilities that will stubbornly resist them, just like happened with the industrial revolution, and then with calculators and computers.

If this is true, then the next AI era will be amazing: every foot soldier becomes a manager, and every manager becomes a general. Tasks like strategic thinking, creativity, reasoning demanding emotional intelligence, other tasks that are not a good fit for the "define it through billions of examples" approach, historically the domain of powerful leaders, will become what *everyone* is doing. And we will almost certainly get wonderful things like very cheap food, vibe-coded 3D-printed big houses (in cities or in nature) for everyone, cures for all diseases and aging, while the world remains fundamentally a recognizable place, with recognizable political and economic structure, and a recognizable leading role for human agency.

My concern is that I am simply not certain that there actually is a significant range of human capabilities that will stubbornly resist LLMs plus the various attachments to them that we will continue to figure out. The utopia story might be true, it might not. There's a big gamble we're taking on the future.

thread_tweets.text:
What do the "last days of human dominance" look like?

(This is assuming that a *permanent* global stop to AI development does not happen)

First, recall my definition of AGI:

AGI is AI that is powerful enough that if you were to upload it into robot bodies, and all humans suddenly disappeared, it would be able to independently continue civilization.

Why this definition, and not something simpler like "AI that can do 90% of economic tasks at median human level"? Because I think this definition captures the core thing that makes AGI "not just a tool", that make it uniquely powerful and unsettling:

In a "on-the-ground" near-term sense, it means that no specific single job is truly safe from automation. Some jobs might have temporary human advantage, but if they needed to, AIs could eventually figure their way around anything.

But also in a more high-level megapolitical sense, once we have AGI, the fact that it's humans who are masters of the Earth, and not robots, becomes a mere historical contingency (we have the property rights and the master keys to the guns, bots don't), and not an inherent result of bots having any fundamental limitation that we do not have. Even if technology were to advance no further, over centuries and millennia as the world continues to do a random walk constantly restructuring its political organization, there is no guarantee that eventually bots would not be on top - and then win permanently due to their greater ability to mass produce themselves. So AGI, defined as I define it, is a "point of no return" that merits great caution.

thread_tweets.text:
But AGI is not the same as ASI (artificial superintelligence). To see the difference, let us zoom into what things might look like one year before the singularity. We also need to add a third line to the chart: the centaur (human + machine).

An analogy here is chess. Machine surpassed the average human in the late 1970s, and surpassed the top human (Kasparov) in 1997. But human + machine continued to beat machine alone all the way until around 2017.

What did the twilight of human + machine in chess look like? Actually, it did look like humans being relegated to more and more "high-level strategic thinking" functions, while bots managed the move-by-move. Very similar to vibe coding in 2026.

Before we get to a point where there are no areas where our input is economically helpful, we will be in a regime where there are only a few such areas. And because we humans are scarce, those few areas will become the global economy's bottleneck - they will be the only thing you can't optimize down to near-zero cost (and latency), and so all our attention will go toward those tasks.

thread_tweets.text:
But here we get to the thing that will confound looking at human and machine as separate units: the possibility of deeper and deeper integration between human and machine.

This is the strand of technology that includes: computers being able to work much more symbiotically with us, picking up on all kinds of conscious and sub-conscious signals we release as an interface, brain-computer interfaces, computer-assisted telepathy that helps multiple people better understand each other and work together. The ultimate end is erasing the binary divide between human and machine and opening up any position on that spectrum. On one end, you sitting at a laptop terminal; on the other end, mind uploading, and also everything in between.

The hope is that deeply integrated human + machine will outperform separated human + machine, and that this will remain the case all the way up to the technological ceiling.

This is the best "far-future AI outcome that I actually like" that I can come up with:

* We preserve global political and economic pluralism (no single company or government dominates) all the way up to the technological ceiling.
* We humans each get the ability to either take technological upgrades to ourselves, and explore the universe at a level competitive with "pure" machines, or stay as we are, and get some ability to act in the world through having a bot listen to our instructions.
* Most of Earth will probably become a highly-regulated nature preserve where anyone can continue living the kind of life they have now (without the pains and annoyances; most challenge will be healthy "good challenge"), and if you want to do more crazy things, you go to space.

Make no mistake - this is a ride through a narrow corridor. Things could go off the rails in one of several different ways: AI-on-its-own could get too powerful relative to the alternatives; one country or one company could gain decisive advantage and decide to lock it in permanently, powerful technological ways of destroying each other could cause incalculable harm, or the human + machine integration approach itself ends up preserving not enough of our humanity, and so it ends up being "just another way to make powerful bots".

For these reasons, I wish that we can ride through the narrow corridor at 60 kph and not 200 kph, which is why I feel warmly toward "slowdown" / "pause" proposals (including "give us a chance to hit the brakes" ideas like mutually assured compute destruction (as in AI 2040, also previously Dan Hendrycks, and others)).

My ideal world is actually one where (i) open weights wins, and (ii) Dean Ball turns out to be completely right that open weights is decelerationist because it deters capex in frontier models [though it will enable more capex in peripherals and applications], because then we get slowdown without any of the downsides of doing it through heavier-handed political means.

But also I don't think we should count on the world to naturally give us "convenient" laws of economics and physics. We do have some degree of agency over the laws of politics, plenty of ways to change incentives without putting a king in charge, and we should exercise that.

But AGI is not the same as ASI (artificial superintelligence). To see the difference, let us zoom into what things might look like one year before the singularity. We also need to add a third line to the chart: the centaur (human + machine).

An analogy here is chess. Machine surpassed the average human in the late 1970s, and surpassed the top human (Kasparov) in 1997. But human + machine continued to beat machine alone all the way until around 2017.

What did the twilight of human + machine in chess look like? Actually, it did look like humans being relegated to more and more "high-level strategic thinking" functions, while bots managed the move-by-move. Very similar to vibe coding in 2026.

Before we get to a point where there are no areas where our input is economically helpful, we will be in a regime where there are only a few such areas. And because we humans are scarce, those few areas will become the global economy's bottleneck - they will be the only thing you can't optimize down to near-zero cost (and latency), and so all our attention will go toward those tasks.

But here we get to the thing that will confound looking at human and machine as separate units: the possibility of deeper and deeper integration between human and machine.

This is the strand of technology that includes: computers being able to work much more symbiotically with us, picking up on all kinds of conscious and sub-conscious signals we release as an interface, brain-computer interfaces, computer-assisted telepathy that helps multiple people better understand each other and work together. The ultimate end is erasing the binary divide between human and machine and opening up any position on that spectrum. On one end, you sitting at a laptop terminal; on the other end, mind uploading, and also everything in between.

The hope is that deeply integrated human + machine will outperform separated human + machine, and that this will remain the case all the way up to the technological ceiling.

This is the best "far-future AI outcome that I actually like" that I can come up with:

* We preserve global political and economic pluralism (no single company or government dominates) all the way up to the technological ceiling.
* We humans each get the ability to either take technological upgrades to ourselves, and explore the universe at a level competitive with "pure" machines, or stay as we are, and get some ability to act in the world through having a bot listen to our instructions.
* Most of Earth will probably become a highly-regulated nature preserve where anyone can continue living the kind of life they have now (without the pains and annoyances; most challenge will be healthy "good challenge"), and if you want to do more crazy things, you go to space.

Make no mistake - this is a ride through a narrow corridor. Things could go off the rails in one of several different ways: AI-on-its-own could get too powerful relative to the alternatives; one country or one company could gain decisive advantage and decide to lock it in permanently, powerful technological ways of destroying each other could cause incalculable harm, or the human + machine integration approach itself ends up preserving not enough of our humanity, and so it ends up being "just another way to make powerful bots".

For these reasons, I wish that we can ride through the narrow corridor at 60 kph and not 200 kph, which is why I feel warmly toward "slowdown" / "pause" proposals (including "give us a chance to hit the brakes" ideas like mutually assured compute destruction (as in AI 2040, also previously Dan Hendrycks, and others)).

My ideal world is actually one where (i) open weights wins, and (ii) Dean Ball turns out to be completely right that open weights is decelerationist because it deters capex in frontier models [though it will enable more capex in peripherals and applications], because then we get slowdown without any of the downsides of doing it through heavier-handed political means.

But also I don't think we should count on the world to naturally give us "convenient" laws of economics and physics. We do have some degree of agency over the laws of politics, plenty of ways to change incentives without putting a king in charge, and we should exercise that.

Chamath: Tokenmaxxing on the top frontier models is “a bridge to nowhere” and a “money-burning furnace”

@chamath reacting to Ramp CEO @eglyman on @CNBC saying:

-- Avg. token spend on @tryramp increased 21x over the last 12 months

-- So, the team built a tool for customers to track it

“He's saying something so important there, because if your engineers are going off randomly in an unguided system and then just ripping through a million tokens at $56, what he's talking about is the eventual downstream impact to earnings.

And that eventually, a bunch of these public market CFOs are gonna show up to Wall Street and they will have missed earnings because their OpEx, at some point, if things are 21x-ing every few months, somebody's going to miss a quarter.

Eric would not have released this Ramp product unless CFOs were like, ‘I can't control the spend.’ And then he's like, ‘Well, here, let me build it for you.’

And then, if enough CFOs essentially turn that feature on and start to rate limit how it's spent, because maybe they're not getting the ROI, and the engineer doesn't care about ROI.

The engineer's like, ‘I want to use the latest, greatest model.’

And maybe you don't need it. Maybe Mira's right, and for 95% of the tasks you should be at one level lower, especially when it costs 1/100th of the cost.

Unless you get a control of this and you can directly say how much money you're making, this is a bridge to nowhere. It is a money-burning furnace.”

tweets.text:
I was asked, how do I manage stress from economic shocks, geopolitical shocks, and all the things that are going on?

While my typical approaches like "pain plus reflection equals progress" and meditation are highly effective, I’ll pass along another principle that I think is the best advice: The Serenity Prayer.

I view this almost like a game. These macro things are happening, but there are also a lot of joyous things and choices that exist with us. It’s really up to me to navigate them.

What can I control that's really important to me? What can't I control? I can't live forever, so I’m not going to get stressed out about that. But on the other hand, I can control certain things. Having that equanimity allows me to approach life in a way where I don't get hijacked.

It is logical, real, and practical. Look around you. You’ve got wonderful things and things you aspire to. Don't get so attached. If you navigate your life well, you're going to be fine.

Kimi K3 feels like a real inflection point. Potentially painful for Anthropic and OpenAI, but a clear net positive for almost everyone else in the stack.

A world with just 2-3 closed frontier labs isn’t healthy. They’d monopsonise chips, power, data centres and eventually swallow apps too.

Anything that increases competition, AI usage and inference workloads, whether strong open-source like K3 or vertically integrated players, spreads the value across the entire ecosystem: infrastructure, neoclouds and software builders.

tweets.text:
We’re entering a world of verifiability as a service.

Anything cheap and fast to verify can be attacked by AI with absurd brut force. It can run millions of experiments in parallel, log every result in markdown/csv, update its hypotheses, and keep searching until something survives verification.

That is why math and software are moving so fast. The feedback loop is immediate and almost free.

Some domains are harder like biology. You cannot instantly verify what happens inside a living cell, an organ, or a human body. Experiments are slow, noisy, expensive, and sometimes impossible to repeat.

The next frontier is not only better intelligence but is making more of reality cheaply and reliably verifiable. The fields that build the best verification loops will accelerate the fastest. A lot of startups will provide this « verifiability as a service » for hard sciences.
